title: First-Time Buyers Crushed as Mortgage Rates Surge Amid Geopolitical Turmoil
description: More than 200 low-deposit mortgage deals have vanished since early March as UK borrowing costs skyrocket following the outbreak of the US-Israel war with Iran.
lede: First-time homebuyers are bearing the brunt of a volatile mortgage market, with more than 200 low-deposit deals disappearing since early March. Borrowing costs are surging as the fallout from the US-Israel war with Iran upends expectations of interest rate cuts, leaving buyers scrambling to secure affordable fixed-rate mortgages.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Mortgage lenders are rapidly pulling products and hiking rates, creating severe upheaval for first-time buyers. Over 200 mortgage deals tailored for buyers with a 5% deposit have been withdrawn from the market since March 6. This past Saturday marked the largest single-day withdrawal of such deals since the 2022 mini-Budget, with 52 products pulled, followed by another 30 on Tuesday.

The cost of borrowing has spiked sharply for those who can still find a low-deposit offer. The average interest rate on a two-year fixed mortgage requiring a 5% deposit has climbed past 6%. For a buyer borrowing £250,000 over 25 years, this translates to an additional £1,200 per year compared to equivalent deals available at the start of the month.

Brokers report that lenders are struggling to price their products amid the chaos, resulting in rate increases coming "thick and fast." Aaron Strutt of Trinity Financial noted that the most competitive rates currently have a shelf life of just three or four days, leaving borrowers with minimal time to make financial decisions.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The sudden surge in mortgage rates has abruptly reversed the trajectory of the UK housing market. Before the onset of the US-Israel war with Iran, financial markets largely anticipated that UK interest rates would be cut this year. This expectation had been driving down lenders' funding costs and reducing rates on new fixed mortgages.

That optimism has been completely upended by geopolitical instability. Overall average rates are climbing across the board, not just for first-time buyers. The average rate on a two-year fixed deal now stands at 5.51%, up from 4.83% at the start of March—its highest point since February of the previous year. Similarly, five-year fixed rates have risen to 5.52%, the highest since July 2024.

The volatility is forcing thousands of prospective buyers out of the market. With more than a fifth of all mortgage products available at the start of the month now withdrawn, the dream of homeownership is becoming increasingly unattainable for those without substantial existing capital.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The mortgage market chaos is deeply tied to shifting expectations regarding the Bank of England's base rate. Turbulence in financial markets has been driven by the assumption that the central bank will implement multiple rate hikes this year to counteract economic shocks from the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

However, there is a stark divergence between market reactions and the views of economic experts. Many economists remain highly skeptical that the Bank of England will aggressively raise the benchmark rate. Governor Andrew Bailey indicated last week that the Monetary Policy Committee, which recently held rates at 3.75%, will carefully "assess how events unfold" rather than rushing to hike rates.

While comments from US President Donald Trump regarding "constructive" talks with Iran provided a brief moment of calm for the markets, it was short-lived. The underlying uncertainty continues to drive up bond yields, simultaneously increasing mortgage costs while boosting returns for those purchasing pension annuities.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Borrowers are being warned to brace for continued volatility. David Hollingworth of L&C Mortgages stated that the market faces a "turbulent period" until the situation in the Middle East becomes clearer. He noted that any easing in the conflict could help the market stabilize and find a predictable level for long-term interest rates.

In the immediate term, financial information service Moneyfacts has advised borrowers to seek independent advice to navigate the ongoing "mortgage mayhem." With deals disappearing overnight, buyers will need to act decisively and remain agile to secure financing in a rapidly shifting landscape.
